title: What grit to smooth plywood edges?
problem: Plywood edges can feel rough, splintery, or uneven before finishing.
likely_cause: Layered plywood edges need coarser smoothing first, then finer grits to reduce splinters.
recommended_grit: Use 120 for rough edges, then 180 and 220 before paint or finish.
steps: Remove dust before finish or edge sealing.
avoid: Do not over-round plywood edges unless that is the intended shape.
success_check: The edge feels smooth with no loose splinters.
quick_answer: For plywood edges, use 120 first, then 180 and 220 before finishing.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand across thin face veneer aggressively.
Do not finish over loose splinters.
search_phrases: what grit to smooth plywood edges

title: What grit for particle board before painting?
problem: Particle board can chip, fuzz, or absorb paint unevenly.
likely_cause: Particle board is softer and more fragile than solid wood, so aggressive sanding can damage the surface.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for light prep, then 320 after primer.
avoid: Do not wet sand raw particle board.
success_check: The surface is smooth, sealed, and ready for paint.
quick_answer: For particle board, sand dry and lightly with 180 or 220, then use 320 after primer.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use coarse grit that tears the surface.
Do not paint unsealed swollen edges.
search_phrases: what grit for particle board before painting

title: What grit for bamboo before oil?
problem: Bamboo can feel slick, uneven, or fuzzy before oiling.
likely_cause: Bamboo is dense and can burnish if sanded too fine before oil, while coarse scratches can show.
recommended_grit: Use 180, then 220 before oiling. Use 320 only for a very light final touch.
avoid: Do not over-polish bamboo before oil.
success_check: The bamboo feels smooth and accepts oil evenly.
quick_answer: For bamboo before oil, sand dry with 180 then 220. Use 320 only lightly.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not wet sand raw bamboo.
Do not leave dust before oiling.
search_phrases: what grit for bamboo before oil

title: What grit for brass polishing prep?
problem: Brass can stay scratched or dull after sanding.
likely_cause: Brass needs fine grit progression before polish can create an even shine.
recommended_grit: Use 600, 800, 1000, 1500, and 2000 before polishing.
avoid: Do not polish over deep 600-grit scratches.
success_check: The brass has an even fine scratch pattern before polishing.
quick_answer: For brass polishing prep, use 600, 800, 1000, 1500, and 2000, then polish.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not skip from 600 directly to polish.
Do not leave metal dust between grits.

title: What grit removes oxidation from copper?
problem: Copper can look dull, stained, or uneven after oxidation.
likely_cause: Oxidation needs removal with a grit that cuts the surface layer before polishing.
recommended_grit: Use 600 for light oxidation, then 800, 1000, and 1500 before polishing.
steps: Polish or protect the copper after sanding.
avoid: Do not start too coarse on decorative copper.
success_check: The oxidation is reduced and the scratch pattern is even.
quick_answer: For copper oxidation, start with 600, then refine with 800, 1000, and 1500 before polishing.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use coarse grit on thin decorative copper.
Do not polish while deep scratches remain.
search_phrases: what grit removes oxidation from copper

title: What grit to scuff powder coated metal?
problem: New paint may not bond well over slick powder coating.
likely_cause: Powder coating is hard and smooth, so the surface must be cleaned and scuffed before repainting.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 to scuff powder coat. Use 600 for finer visible topcoat prep.
steps: Clean grease and residue from the powder coat.
Remove dust before primer or paint.
avoid: Do not paint over glossy powder coat without scuffing.
success_check: The surface is clean, dull, and evenly scuffed.
quick_answer: For powder coated metal, clean first, scuff with 320 or 400, then use 600 for finer prep.

Remove all dust before primer or paint.

title: Can I sand foam?
problem: Foam can tear, crumble, or clog sandpaper when sanded aggressively.
likely_cause: Foam is soft, so coarse grit and heavy pressure can rip the surface instead of smoothing it.

steps: Stop if the foam tears or crumbles.
avoid: Do not use heavy pressure on soft foam.
success_check: The foam is shaped without torn chunks or rough gouges.
quick_answer: For firm foam, use light pressure with 220 or 320, then 400. Soft foam may tear instead of sand cleanly.

title: What grit for drywall corner bead?
problem: Drywall corner bead repair can leave ridges or visible edges after sanding.

title: What grit for spackle pinholes?
